nodes.title: Accidental Adversaries
nodes.oneLiner: Two parties who should be collaborating but whose actions inadvertently undermine each other

nodes.title: Action Bias
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency to do something - anything - rather than wait, watch, and understand

nodes.title: Adaptive Capacity
nodes.oneLiner: The ability to adjust to changing conditions - the resource that matters most when you can't predict what's coming

nodes.title: Adaptive Cycle
nodes.oneLiner: The recurring pattern of growth, conservation, release, and reorganisation that all living systems move through

nodes.title: Antifragility
nodes.oneLiner: Systems that don't just survive shocks but get stronger from them - beyond robust, beyond resilient

nodes.title: Attractors
nodes.oneLiner: The states a system naturally gravitates toward - change the attractor and the system reorganises itself

nodes.title: Boundaries
nodes.oneLiner: Where you draw the line around a system changes everything you see inside it

nodes.title: Boundary Critique
nodes.oneLiner: Asking who decided what's inside and outside this system - because that choice shapes everything that follows

nodes.title: Bounded Rationality
nodes.oneLiner: People make reasonable decisions given the information and cognitive capacity available to them - which is always limited

nodes.title: Buffers
nodes.oneLiner: The slack in a system that absorbs shocks and keeps things stable when conditions change

nodes.title: Carrying Capacity
nodes.oneLiner: The maximum a system can sustain - every system has limits, even if they're not visible yet

nodes.title: Catalytic Mechanisms
nodes.oneLiner: Small structural changes that reliably produce the right behaviour without constant enforcement

nodes.title: Causal Loop Diagrams
nodes.oneLiner: A visual tool for mapping the reinforcing and balancing feedback that drives a system's behaviour

nodes.title: Co-evolution
nodes.oneLiner: When two or more systems evolve in response to each other, neither standing still

nodes.title: Cognitive Load
nodes.oneLiner: The amount of working memory being used - complex systems impose high load, which degrades thinking

nodes.title: Complex Adaptive Systems
nodes.oneLiner: Systems made of many agents that learn, adapt, and produce behaviour nobody designed

nodes.title: Complexity vs Complication
nodes.oneLiner: A jet engine is complicated - predictable if you understand it. Raising a child is complex - fundamentally unpredictable

nodes.title: Constraints
nodes.oneLiner: Limitations that shape behaviour - sometimes adding one creates better outcomes than removing one

nodes.title: Culture as System
nodes.oneLiner: Culture isn't something you install - it's an emergent property of interactions, incentives, and structures

nodes.title: Cynefin Framework
nodes.oneLiner: A sense-making model that matches your response to the kind of situation you're in

nodes.title: Decision Fatigue
nodes.oneLiner: The deteriorating quality of decisions after a long session of decision-making

nodes.title: Delays
nodes.oneLiner: The gap between doing something and seeing what happens - the source of most bad decisions in systems.

nodes.title: Distributed Leadership
nodes.oneLiner: Leadership as a property of the system, not a role - whoever sees the problem leads the response

nodes.title: Diversity and Stability
nodes.oneLiner: More diverse systems tend to be more resilient - monocultures are efficient until they're catastrophically fragile

nodes.title: Double-Loop Learning
nodes.oneLiner: Questioning not just 'are we doing things right?' but 'are we doing the right things?'

nodes.title: Drift
nodes.oneLiner: Slow, invisible movement in a system's behaviour that goes unnoticed until it's dramatic

nodes.title: Dynamic Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: Thinking in terms of change over time rather than frozen snapshots - how does this play out?

nodes.title: Edge of Chaos
nodes.oneLiner: The zone between rigid order and total disorder where systems are most creative and adaptive

nodes.title: Emergence
nodes.oneLiner: When the whole does something none of the parts could do alone - behaviour that arises from interactions, not instructions.

nodes.title: Equilibrium
nodes.oneLiner: A state where opposing forces balance - stable until something shifts the balance

nodes.title: Eroding Goals
nodes.oneLiner: When you lower your standards instead of closing the gap between where you are and where you want to be

nodes.title: Escalation
nodes.oneLiner: Two parties each responding to the other's moves, ratcheting up intensity until both lose

nodes.title: Event-Pattern-Structure
nodes.oneLiner: Moving from 'what happened?' to 'what keeps happening?' to 'what's causing the pattern?'

nodes.title: Exponential Growth
nodes.oneLiner: When something grows by a percentage rather than an amount - it feels slow until it suddenly feels unstoppable.

nodes.title: Feedback loops
nodes.oneLiner: Reinforcing loops accelerate change, balancing loops resist it - together they drive all system behaviour.

nodes.title: Feedback Starvation
nodes.oneLiner: When a system lacks the information it needs to self-correct - common in hierarchies where bad news doesn't travel upward

nodes.title: Fixes that fail
nodes.oneLiner: A quick fix that addresses the symptom but makes the underlying problem worse over time.

nodes.title: Goodhart's Law
nodes.oneLiner: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure

nodes.title: Groupthink
nodes.oneLiner: When the desire for agreement overrides honest thinking - a system failure disguised as alignment

nodes.title: Growth and Underinvestment
nodes.oneLiner: Growth creates demand, but investment in capacity doesn't keep up - so performance drops and justifies not investing

nodes.title: Hindsight Bias
nodes.oneLiner: After something happens, it seems obvious it was going to happen - making us overconfident about our ability to predict

nodes.title: Holding Space
nodes.oneLiner: Creating conditions for emergence rather than controlling outcomes - leadership as environment design

nodes.title: Iceberg Model
nodes.oneLiner: Events are the tip - below the waterline sit the patterns, structures, and mental models that create them

nodes.title: Institutional Inertia
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency of large systems to resist change, even when everyone agrees change is needed

nodes.title: Interconnections
nodes.oneLiner: The relationships between parts matter more than the parts themselves

nodes.title: Intervention Side Effects
nodes.oneLiner: Every action in a complex system produces effects beyond the one you intended

nodes.title: Irreducibility
nodes.oneLiner: Some systems can only be understood by running them - there's no shortcut to the answer

nodes.title: Keystone Species
nodes.oneLiner: The role whose removal would fundamentally alter the system - the node that holds everything together

nodes.title: Leading vs Lagging Indicators
nodes.oneLiner: Lagging indicators tell you what happened. Leading indicators tell you what's coming. Most people only track the first kind

nodes.title: Learning Organisation
nodes.oneLiner: An organisation that continuously transforms itself by learning from its own experience

nodes.title: Leverage Points
nodes.oneLiner: The places in a system where a small change can shift everything

nodes.title: Limits to Growth
nodes.oneLiner: Every growing system eventually hits a constraint - the question is whether you see it coming

nodes.title: Linear Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: Assuming that cause and effect are proportional, direct, and one-directional - the default that fails in complex systems

nodes.title: Local vs Global Optimisation
nodes.oneLiner: What's best for the part isn't always best for the whole - the tension at the heart of most system design

nodes.title: Lock-in
nodes.oneLiner: When path dependence becomes a trap - the cost of switching is so high that inferior solutions persist

nodes.title: Map is Not the Territory
nodes.oneLiner: Every model is a simplification - useful but never the whole truth

nodes.title: Mental Models
nodes.oneLiner: The invisible assumptions and stories we carry about how the world works

nodes.title: Minimum Viable Intervention
nodes.oneLiner: The smallest change that could shift the system - start small, learn fast

nodes.title: Modularity
nodes.oneLiner: Systems made of loosely connected parts that can fail independently without bringing down the whole

nodes.title: Motivated Reasoning
nodes.oneLiner: Finding the evidence for what you already believe - the human default, not the exception

nodes.title: Multiple Perspectives
nodes.oneLiner: Any complex system looks different depending on where you stand - no single viewpoint is complete

nodes.title: Mutualism
nodes.oneLiner: Relationships where both parties benefit - rare in rhetoric, common in reality

nodes.title: Narrative Fallacy
nodes.oneLiner: The need to construct a simple story from complex events - makes us feel like we understand, even when we don't

nodes.title: Nested Systems
nodes.oneLiner: Every system is simultaneously a whole and a part of something larger - teams within organisations within sectors within societies

nodes.title: Network Effects
nodes.oneLiner: When a product or system becomes more valuable as more people use it - the force behind platforms and standards

nodes.title: Niche Construction
nodes.oneLiner: Organisms and organisations don't just adapt to their environment - they reshape it

nodes.title: Nonlinearity
nodes.oneLiner: Small changes can have huge effects, and big changes can have none - systems rarely respond in proportion

nodes.title: Nudges
nodes.oneLiner: Subtle changes to the choice environment that steer behaviour without restricting options

nodes.title: Observer Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The act of measuring or watching a system changes how the system behaves

nodes.title: Organisational Debt
nodes.oneLiner: The accumulated cost of shortcuts, workarounds, and deferred structural decisions

nodes.title: Oscillation
nodes.oneLiner: Systems that overshoot and undershoot because feedback arrives too late

nodes.title: Outcome Mapping
nodes.oneLiner: Tracking changes in behaviour and relationships rather than counting outputs - designed for complex change

nodes.title: Overshoot and Collapse
nodes.oneLiner: When a system blows past its sustainable limits before feedback kicks in, and the correction is catastrophic

nodes.title: Panarchy
nodes.oneLiner: Adaptive cycles nested across scales - what happens at one level affects cycles above and below

nodes.title: Parasitism
nodes.oneLiner: Relationships where one party extracts value at the other's expense - sometimes structural, not intentional

nodes.title: Participatory Systems Mapping
nodes.oneLiner: Building system maps with the people who live in the system, not just the people who study it

nodes.title: Path Dependence
nodes.oneLiner: Where you can go depends on where you've been - history constrains future options

nodes.title: Perverse Incentives
nodes.oneLiner: Incentive structures that reward the opposite of what you intended - the cobra effect

nodes.title: Policy Resistance
nodes.oneLiner: When a system fights back against your attempt to change it

nodes.title: Portfolio of Experiments
nodes.oneLiner: Running multiple small interventions simultaneously to increase the chance of finding what works

nodes.title: Power in Systems
nodes.oneLiner: Who sets the rules, who controls the flows, who defines success - structure IS power

nodes.title: Probe-Sense-Respond
nodes.oneLiner: The strategy for complex situations - try something small, see what happens, then decide what to do next

nodes.title: Proxy Measures
nodes.oneLiner: Using something measurable as a stand-in for something that isn't - useful until people start gaming the proxy

nodes.title: Punctuated Equilibrium
nodes.oneLiner: Long periods of stability interrupted by sudden, dramatic change - how most systems evolve in practice

nodes.title: Reductionism
nodes.oneLiner: Breaking things into parts to understand them - powerful for machines, misleading for living systems

nodes.title: Redundancy
nodes.oneLiner: Having more than you strictly need - looks wasteful in stable times, looks essential in a crisis

nodes.title: Regime Shifts
nodes.oneLiner: When a system flips from one stable state to another - often suddenly, usually hard to reverse

nodes.title: Requisite Variety
nodes.oneLiner: To stay in control of a system, you need at least as many responses as the system has ways of changing

nodes.title: Resilience
nodes.oneLiner: The ability to absorb disturbance and still maintain essential function - not bouncing back, but holding together

nodes.title: Robustness vs Resilience
nodes.oneLiner: Robustness resists change like a fortress. Resilience absorbs and adapts like a reed. Different strategies for different worlds

nodes.title: Rule Beating
nodes.oneLiner: People find ways to comply with the letter of a rule while violating its spirit - and the system gets what it measures, not what it wants

nodes.title: S-Curves
nodes.oneLiner: Growth that starts slowly, accelerates, and then levels off as it hits limits - the shape of most real change

nodes.title: Safe-to-Fail Experiments
nodes.oneLiner: Small probes designed to test how a complex system responds - if they fail, the damage is contained

nodes.title: Satisficing
nodes.oneLiner: Choosing good enough rather than optimal - often the rational response to complexity

nodes.title: Scale Effects
nodes.oneLiner: Things that work at one scale often don't work at another - what succeeds in a startup fails in a corporation, and vice versa

nodes.title: Second-Order Effects
nodes.oneLiner: The consequences of the consequences - what happens after the first thing happens

nodes.title: Self-Organisation
nodes.oneLiner: Order that arises from interactions, not instructions - nobody planned it but it works

nodes.title: Sense-Analyse-Respond
nodes.oneLiner: The strategy for complicated situations - gather data, analyse it, then act on what the experts find

nodes.title: Sensemaking Under Pressure
nodes.oneLiner: How people actually make sense of complex situations in real time - usually not through analysis

nodes.title: Sensemaking
nodes.oneLiner: The process of creating meaning from ambiguous, complex situations - not decision-making, but what comes before it

nodes.title: Sensitivity to Initial Conditions
nodes.oneLiner: Tiny differences at the start can produce vastly different outcomes - and you can't always tell which ones matter

nodes.title: Shifting the Burden
nodes.oneLiner: Relying on a quick fix so much that the ability to do the real thing slowly disappears.

nodes.title: Signal vs Noise
nodes.oneLiner: The challenge of distinguishing meaningful information from random variation - most of what looks like signal is noise

nodes.title: Silos
nodes.oneLiner: When parts of a system optimise independently, losing sight of the whole - a structural problem, not a people problem

nodes.title: Solutionism
nodes.oneLiner: The assumption that every problem has a clean solution - when complex systems often need navigation, not fixing

nodes.title: Stakeholder Mapping
nodes.oneLiner: Identifying everyone who affects or is affected by a system - including those with no voice at the table

nodes.title: Stocks and Flows
nodes.oneLiner: Everything accumulates or depletes - and the difference between the two drives how systems behave

nodes.title: Sub-optimisation
nodes.oneLiner: Optimising one part of a system at the expense of the whole - every department hitting its targets while the organisation fails

nodes.title: Success to the Successful
nodes.oneLiner: Winners get more resources, which makes them win more - a structural advantage that compounds over time

nodes.title: Succession
nodes.oneLiner: The predictable sequence of change after a disturbance - cleared ground doesn't stay cleared

nodes.title: Surrogate Measures
nodes.oneLiner: When you can't measure what matters, you measure what you can - and then forget the difference

nodes.title: Symbiosis
nodes.oneLiner: Different organisms - or organisations - living in close association, for better or worse

nodes.title: System Blindness
nodes.oneLiner: The inability to see systemic causes - defaulting to blaming individuals when the structure is the problem

nodes.title: System Traps
nodes.oneLiner: Predictable structural failures that show up across every kind of system

nodes.title: Systemic Design
nodes.oneLiner: Combining systems thinking with design practice - understanding the system before designing the intervention

nodes.title: Systems Mapping
nodes.oneLiner: The practice of making a system visible - its parts, flows, boundaries, and dynamics

nodes.title: Theory of Change
nodes.oneLiner: A map of how you believe change happens - from activities to outcomes - only useful if it acknowledges complexity

nodes.title: Tipping Points
nodes.oneLiner: The moment when a gradual change suddenly becomes a dramatic, often irreversible shift

nodes.title: Tragedy of the Commons
nodes.oneLiner: When everyone acting in their own interest destroys the shared resource they all depend on

nodes.title: Transformability
nodes.oneLiner: The capacity to create a fundamentally new system when the existing one is no longer viable

nodes.title: Transition Management
nodes.oneLiner: A governance approach for guiding large-scale system transitions over long timeframes

nodes.title: Uncertainty vs Risk
nodes.oneLiner: Risk is when you know the odds. Uncertainty is when you don't even know what could happen

nodes.title: Unintended Consequences
nodes.oneLiner: Every intervention in a system produces effects you didn't plan for - sometimes bigger than the ones you did.

nodes.title: Weak Signals
nodes.oneLiner: Early, ambiguous indicators of emerging change - easy to dismiss, potentially critical

nodes.title: Wicked Problems
nodes.oneLiner: Problems that resist definition, change shape when you try to solve them, and have no stopping rule

edges.note: "Each party's well-intentioned actions trigger a reinforcing loop that erodes the partnership"
"Each party draws their boundary around their own goals, missing how their actions affect the other"
"The adversarial dynamic is entirely unintended - both parties want the partnership to work"
"Accidental Adversaries can tip into Escalation when each party starts retaliating against the other's unintended harm"
"Action bias feeds solutionism - the urge to act prevents accepting that some situations need navigation, not fixing"
"Many fixes that fail are driven by action bias - the need to do something overrides the need to understand the situation first"
"Action bias increases intervention side effects because the action is taken before the system is understood"
"Probe-sense-respond channels action bias productively - it allows action but makes it small and learning-oriented"
"Action bias makes people react to noise - the urge to act doesn't distinguish between meaningful change and random variation"
"Adaptive capacity is what makes resilience possible - without the ability to adjust, a system can only break or resist"
"Adaptive capacity is requisite variety in practice - the range of responses available when things change"
"Adaptive capacity is the defining resource of complex adaptive systems - what allows them to evolve rather than collapse"
"Under genuine uncertainty, adaptive capacity matters more than prediction - you can't plan for what you can't foresee"
"Redundancy is a key source of adaptive capacity - spare resources that can be redeployed when conditions change"
"Transformability goes beyond adaptive capacity - not adjusting within the current system, but creating a fundamentally new one"
"A learning organisation builds adaptive capacity continuously - organisational learning is how adaptability grows"
"Diversity builds adaptive capacity - if one approach fails, alternatives already exist within the system"
"Each phase of the adaptive cycle has different resilience - highest in early growth, lowest in late conservation"
"Panarchy is adaptive cycles nested across scales - the release phase at one level can trigger reorganisation at another"
"The reorganisation phase of the adaptive cycle is the system at the edge of chaos - maximum potential, minimum structure"
"The growth phase of the adaptive cycle follows an S-curve - rapid early growth that slows as the system matures"
"The conservation phase creates path dependence - accumulated structure constrains future options"
"Regime shifts often correspond to the release phase - when accumulated rigidity in the conservation phase finally breaks"
"Transformability is most relevant in the release and reorganisation phases, when the old system has broken and new possibilities exist"
"The adaptive cycle is the internal mechanism behind punctuated equilibrium - what happens during both stability and disruption"
"Inertia builds during the conservation phase - the system becomes rigid and resistant as it matures"
"Succession is the growth and reorganisation phases of the adaptive cycle playing out in sequence after disturbance"
"Transition management works with the adaptive cycle - supporting natural phases rather than fighting them"
"Resilience absorbs shocks and persists. Antifragility goes further - the system improves because of the shock"
"Antifragility extends the spectrum: fragile breaks under stress, robust resists it, resilient recovers from it, antifragile gains from it"
"Antifragile systems thrive at the edge of chaos - enough disorder to trigger adaptation, not so much that it destroys"
"Complex adaptive systems can be antifragile - the stress of environmental change drives learning and evolution"
"Antifragility requires feedback loops that translate stress into improvement - without feedback, stress is just damage"
"Safe-to-fail experiments build antifragility - controlled stressors that make the system learn and grow stronger"
"A portfolio approach is antifragile - each experiment that fails teaches the system something, making it stronger"
"Balancing feedback loops create attractors by pulling the system back toward a particular state"
"Tipping points mark the boundary between two attractors - once crossed, the system falls toward a different stable state"
"Drift can slowly shift which attractor a system is closest to, until it tips into a new one"
"Attractors emerge from the web of interconnections - change the relationships and the attractor changes"
"Self-organising systems often settle into attractor states - stable patterns that emerge from agent interactions"
"A regime shift is a move from one attractor to another - the system finds a new stable state with different dynamics"
"Boundaries cut across interconnections - every boundary hides some relationships and reveals others"
"Unintended consequences often come from drawing the boundary too tightly and missing what's outside it"
"Emergent behaviour can only be seen when the boundary is drawn wide enough to include the interacting parts"
"Drawing a boundary too narrowly can cut a feedback loop in half, making the behaviour inside the boundary look random"
"Constraints and boundaries both define what's inside and outside - what the system can and can't do"
"Wicked problems resist boundary-drawing - every definition of the problem leaves out something that matters"
"Mental models determine where we draw boundaries - shaping what counts as 'the problem' and what stays invisible"
"Every systems map begins with a boundary decision - what's inside the map and what's left out"
"Boundary critique makes boundary choices visible and negotiable - every boundary is a decision that shapes what you see"
"Different perspectives draw different boundaries - where you stand determines what you include in the system"
"Boundary-drawing is an exercise of power - whoever defines the system decides whose interests count"
"Sub-optimisation happens when boundaries are drawn too tightly around parts, losing sight of the whole"
"Nested systems show that every boundary is also a connection - the edge of one system is the interface with the next"
"Silos are boundaries that have hardened into barriers - necessary separation that became isolation"
"Different perspectives draw different boundaries - boundary critique makes those differences visible and negotiable"
"Whoever draws the boundary holds power - they decide what counts as relevant and what gets excluded"
"Wicked problems resist boundary-drawing - every boundary excludes something that turns out to matter"
"Boundaries reflect mental models - how you frame the system reveals what you assume about it"
"Systemic design begins with boundary critique - questioning what's included in the design brief and what's been left out"
"Satisficing is the rational response to bounded rationality - choosing good enough when optimal is unknowable"
"Mental models are how bounded rationality works in practice - we simplify the world to make decisions possible"
"Bounded rationality explains why complex systems are so hard - our cognitive capacity is bounded, the system's complexity isn't"
"System blindness is partly a consequence of bounded rationality - we default to simple explanations because the systemic ones exceed our processing capacity"
"Proxy measures exist because of bounded rationality - we measure what we can process, not what we ideally would"
"Buffers are stocks - they accumulate reserves that smooth out uneven flows"
"Buffers buy time, giving you space to respond before a delay in the system causes damage"
"Without buffers, feedback loops react to every fluctuation, making the system jittery and unstable"
"Removing a buffer can push a system past a threshold it would otherwise have absorbed"
"Buffers give you room to work within constraints - the slack that makes tight systems survivable"
"Buffers provide variety in the time dimension - more options for when and how to respond"
"Buffers are a key source of resilience - the slack that absorbs shocks before they reach critical system functions"
"Buffers are a form of redundancy in stocks - excess capacity that smooths out volatility and absorbs shocks"
"Carrying capacity is the limit that limits-to-growth hits - the ceiling the system can't sustainably exceed"
"Overshoot happens when a system exceeds its carrying capacity before feedback kicks in"
"Carrying capacity is determined by the balance of stocks and flows - what the system can replenish as fast as it's consumed"
"Buffers can temporarily mask the approach to carrying capacity - the system feels fine until the buffer is exhausted"
"Exponential growth makes carrying capacity dangerous - the system can go from 'plenty of room' to 'exceeded' in very few cycles"
"Catalytic mechanisms are a form of high-leverage intervention - small structural changes with outsized effects"
"The best catalytic mechanisms work by creating new feedback loops that make the desired behaviour self-reinforcing"
"Catalytic mechanisms sidestep policy resistance by aligning the system's interests rather than overriding them"
"Well-designed catalytic mechanisms can break system traps by changing the structural incentives that create them"
"Where fixes that fail push against the system, catalytic mechanisms reshape the system so the right behaviour is the easy behaviour"
"Nudges preserve freedom of choice; catalytic mechanisms often work by making the right choice the only practical one"
"Many catalytic mechanisms work as enabling constraints - limiting options in ways that improve outcomes"
"Well-designed catalytic mechanisms avoid perverse incentives by aligning the mechanism tightly with the actual goal"
"Causal loop diagrams exist to make feedback loops visible - they're the notation system for the concept"
"Causal loop diagrams are one of the most common tools in the broader practice of systems mapping"
"CLDs make interconnections visible and traceable - showing not just that things are connected, but how"
"Drawing a CLD surfaces mental models - it forces you to state your assumptions about what causes what"
"CLDs help you see below the event layer of the iceberg, revealing the feedback structures that produce patterns"
"Co-evolution is what happens when complex adaptive systems interact - each one's adaptation changes the other's environment"
"Co-evolution is driven by feedback loops between systems - each change triggers a response that triggers another change"
"Escalation is co-evolution's destructive form - two parties adapting to each other in ways that ratchet up intensity"
"Co-evolution produces emergent outcomes that neither system would have produced alone"
"Co-evolving systems self-organise in response to each other, creating patterns neither designed"
"Symbiotic partners co-evolve - each adapts to the other, deepening the interdependence"
"Niche construction is one-directional co-evolution - reshaping the environment changes the selection pressures"
"Cognitive load is the mechanism behind bounded rationality - the bounds are set by how much the mind can process at once"
"Sustained cognitive load produces decision fatigue - the system degrades under prolonged demand"
"Complex systems impose high cognitive load because the interactions can't be simplified without losing essential information"
"High cognitive load drives satisficing - when processing capacity is full, good enough replaces optimal"
"High cognitive load makes linear thinking more likely - the mind defaults to simple causal chains when overloaded"
"Emergence is the defining property of complex adaptive systems - behaviour arises from interaction, not instruction"
"Complex adaptive systems self-organise - order appears without anyone designing it"
"Complex adaptive systems are the archetype of what makes complexity different from complication"
"Agents in a CAS learn through feedback loops - adjusting their behaviour based on what happens"
"CAS are inherently nonlinear - small changes in agent behaviour can produce system-wide shifts"
"Complex adaptive systems operate most effectively at the edge of chaos - the zone between rigidity and dissolution"
"Centralised leadership can't keep pace with a complex adaptive system - distributed leadership matches the system's speed"
"Emergence is what makes complex systems complex - behaviour arises that can't be predicted from the parts"
"Complicated systems may have feedback loops, but complex ones have feedback loops that interact, adapt, and surprise"
"Complication can be linear - more parts, more steps. Complexity is inherently nonlinear"
"In complicated systems, the right lever is findable. In complex ones, leverage points shift and hide"
"Complicated systems have predictable delays. Complex ones have delays that interact with other delays in unpredictable ways"
"Cynefin builds on this distinction, adding chaotic and confused domains to the simple-complicated-complex map"
"Wicked problems are what happens when complex systems generate challenges that resist every tame-problem approach"
"Complicated systems generate quantifiable risk. Complex ones generate genuine uncertainty"
"Sensitivity to initial conditions is one of the key markers of complexity - complicated systems are usually robust to small starting differences"
"Irreducibility is the deepest distinction - complicated systems can be shortcut, complex ones often can't"
"The mental model that everything is a machine is what makes people treat complex problems as merely complicated"
"Reductionism works for complicated systems and fails for complex ones - the interactions that define complexity disappear when you take things apart"
"Solutionism is what happens when someone applies the complicated mindset to complex problems - assuming a clean answer exists"
"Robustness suits complicated environments where threats are known. Resilience suits complex ones where surprises are guaranteed"
"Safe-to-fail is the right approach for complex problems - where you can't predict the outcome, you make the trying safe"
"The observer effect is a hallmark of complex systems - the system responds to being studied, unlike a machine"
"Sensemaking is most critical in complex situations where the relationship between cause and effect is unclear"
"Outcome mapping was designed for complex change - where you can influence behaviour but can't control final outcomes"
"Constraints are what limits to growth hits - every growing system eventually meets one"
"Adding or removing a constraint can be a high-leverage intervention - it reshapes what the system can do"
"The right constraints hold a system at the edge of chaos - enough structure to function, enough freedom to adapt"
"Robustness works by adding constraints that prevent change. Resilience works by maintaining flexibility to respond to it"
"Culture is an emergent property - it arises from the interactions of many people, not from a mission statement"
"Culture self-organises from the daily interactions, incentives, and norms that people experience"
"Culture is the collective expression of shared mental models - the assumptions that 'everyone knows' but nobody states"
"Culture reinforces itself through feedback loops - the culture shapes behaviour, and behaviour reinforces the culture"
"Culture is one of the strongest sources of institutional inertia - it outlasts every programme designed to change it"
"Culture is niche construction at the social level - people reshape their social environment, which then shapes them back"
"In Cynefin's complex domain, outcomes emerge from interactions rather than being designed"
"Each Cynefin domain requires different feedback strategies - tight loops in complex, analytical loops in complicated"
"Cynefin helps you find leverage by first identifying what kind of system you're dealing with"
"Policy resistance often signals you've misidentified the domain - treating a complex problem with a complicated approach"
"Wicked problems are the signature challenge of Cynefin's complex domain - requiring probes, not plans"
"Cynefin's complex domain describes the territory where complex adaptive systems operate"
"Cynefin's complicated domain involves manageable risk. Its complex and chaotic domains involve genuine uncertainty"
"Safe-to-fail experiments are the primary intervention strategy for Cynefin's complex domain - probe, sense, respond"
"Cynefin is fundamentally a sensemaking framework - its first job is helping you understand what kind of situation you're in"
"Under pressure, people default to familiar domain responses - the risk is applying the wrong domain's approach"
"Probe-sense-respond is the prescribed strategy for Cynefin's complex domain"
"Sense-analyse-respond is the prescribed strategy for Cynefin's complicated domain"
"Decision fatigue is bounded rationality in the time dimension - the bounds narrow as the day progresses"
"Satisficing increases under decision fatigue - as energy depletes, the threshold for 'good enough' drops"
"Decision fatigue can cause drift - small, tired compromises that accumulate into significant departures from intent"
"Tired decision-makers erode their own goals - lowering standards because maintaining them requires energy they've spent"
"Delays in feedback loops are what make systems overshoot and oscillate"
"Delays in recognising exponential growth are why it catches people off guard"
"Stocks change slowly because flows take time - the delay between opening a tap and filling a bath"
"Oscillation is the signature behaviour of delays in balancing loops - overcorrection, then overcorrection again"
"Delays make drift invisible - by the time you notice the standard has slipped, the new normal is already set"
"Delays hide intervention side effects - the damage doesn't show up until long after the change"
"Second-order effects are often hidden by delays - the first consequence is visible, the next ones take time"
"Delays hide the moment when initial conditions mattered - by the time you see the divergence, the cause is long gone"
"Delays compound uncertainty - the longer the gap between action and outcome, the more unknowns accumulate"
"Dynamic thinking makes delays visible by asking 'when does this show up?' not just 'what happens?'"
"Lagging indicators are delayed signals - by the time they move, the cause is already in the past"
"Feedback starvation is an extreme form of delay - the feedback isn't just slow, it never arrives"
"The delay between accumulating organisational debt and feeling its consequences is what makes it so dangerous"
"Distributed leadership is self-organisation applied to how decisions get made and action gets taken"
"Distributed leadership increases requisite variety - more people leading means more diverse responses to challenges"
"Distributed leadership builds resilience - the system doesn't depend on any single leader to function"
"Distributed leadership is emergent - it arises from the situation and the capabilities present, not from the org chart"
"Holding space is what formal leaders do in a distributed model - designing conditions rather than directing action"
"Diversity is one of the primary sources of resilience - more different parts means more ways to respond to disturbance"
"Diversity provides requisite variety - a diverse system has more response options than a uniform one"
"Diversity is a form of functional redundancy - different parts that can serve similar purposes in different conditions"
"Diverse, modular systems are the most resilient - failure in one type doesn't cascade because alternatives exist"
"Double-loop learning is the mechanism that makes a learning organisation genuinely adaptive"
"Double-loop learning targets mental models - it's the process of questioning the assumptions beneath the strategy"
"Single-loop learning addresses events. Double-loop learning reaches the structures and mental models beneath"
"Single-loop learning is a feedback loop that adjusts behaviour. Double-loop learning is a feedback loop that adjusts the rules"
"Single-loop works at the event level. Double-loop reaches the structure and asks whether the structure itself needs changing"
"Groupthink prevents double-loop learning - if nobody questions the assumptions, they never get tested"
"Double-loop learning is the antidote to motivated reasoning - deliberately questioning the assumptions reasoning protects"
"Drift happens when balancing feedback loops weaken - the signal that something is off gets quieter and quieter"
"Drift can carry a system toward a tipping point so gradually that nobody notices until it's crossed"
"Drift often goes unnoticed because people's sense of what's normal shifts along with the change"
"Drift often silently depletes buffers - reserves, goodwill, capacity - until there's nothing left when you need them"
"Eroding Goals is the archetype version of drift applied to standards and targets"
"Drift during equilibrium periods is what makes the punctuation inevitable - slow, invisible erosion building until it breaks"
"Drift is invisible if you only track lagging indicators - the slow change doesn't show until the accumulated effect is dramatic"
"Feedback starvation enables drift - without signals that something is changing, the slow decline goes unnoticed"
"Surrogate displacement happens through drift - the measure replaces the goal so slowly that nobody sees the switch"
"Organisational debt accumulates through drift - each shortcut is imperceptible, the total is dramatic"
"Drift can slowly shift what the system treats as its equilibrium, disguising the change"
"Dynamic thinking is the antidote to linear thinking - replacing the straight-line projection with a sense of how systems behave over time"
"Dynamic thinking follows feedback loops through time - seeing how today's effect becomes tomorrow's cause"
"Stocks and flows are the language of dynamic thinking - what's accumulating, what's draining, and how fast"
"Dynamic thinking is how you catch exponential growth early - by asking 'what does this look like in six months?' not 'what does it look like now?'"
"Leading indicators support dynamic thinking - they tell you about trajectory, not just current position"
"Dynamic thinking helps separate signal from noise - trends over time reveal patterns that single points can't"
"Weak signals show up as subtle changes in trajectory - dynamic thinking makes them easier to spot"
"Self-organisation happens most richly at the edge of chaos - too much order freezes it, too much chaos dissolves it"
"The edge of chaos is far from equilibrium - that's what makes it creative but also fragile"
"The edge of chaos is where tipping points live - the system is poised between states, ready to shift"
"Holding space keeps the system at the edge of chaos - enough structure to contain, enough freedom to create"
"Feedback loops are the mechanism through which emergence often arises"
"Emergent behaviour is often unintended - the system produces what nobody designed"
"Emergence arises from interconnections - behaviour the parts can't produce alone"
"Emergent behaviour is inherently nonlinear - the whole responds in ways the parts don't predict"
"Emergent behaviour often appears suddenly at a tipping point - the system reorganises all at once"
"Self-organisation is emergence at the structural level - the system builds its own order from within"
"Emergent behaviour is often irreducible - you can't predict it from the parts, you have to watch it happen"
"Emergence is what reductionism misses - behaviour that exists only in the interaction, not in the parts"
"You can't design emergence, but you can hold the space where emergence is most likely to happen"
"Balancing feedback loops are the mechanism that maintains equilibrium - they correct deviations and restore balance"
"An equilibrium is a type of attractor - the state the system is pulled toward by its balancing loops"
"Oscillation happens around an equilibrium when the corrections overshoot"
"A tipping point is the moment equilibrium breaks - the system shifts to a new balance point"
"Equilibrium occurs when inflows and outflows are equal - the stock stays constant even though the flows are active"
"A balancing loop should close the gap between performance and the goal - but this archetype weakens the goal instead"
"Delays between performance dropping and the consequences being felt make it easier to lower the goal instead of fixing the performance"
"Lowering the goal is the symptomatic fix - easier than the hard work of closing the performance gap"
"Lowering standards looks like a fix but it erodes the system's long-term capability"
"Falling performance gets normalised, lowering the standard that would have justified investment"
"Rule beating can mask eroding goals - the metrics look fine while the reality deteriorates"
"Eroding goals is a system trap - the slow drift in standards is structural, not a failure of willpower"
"The shift from the real goal to the surrogate is a form of goal erosion - so gradual nobody notices the substitution"
"Organisational debt often involves eroding goals - accepting worse standards because fixing the structure feels too hard"
"Escalation is two reinforcing loops locked together - each party's action feeds the other's response"
"Each escalation step is a fix that fails - it addresses the immediate threat while making the overall situation worse"
"Escalation burns through buffers - money, goodwill, energy - until both sides are depleted"
"Escalation is nonlinear - small provocations can trigger disproportionate responses that spiral"
"Escalation is one of the named system traps - its structure makes it predictable and its exit routes identifiable"
"Each side's escalation is a form of policy resistance against the other's moves"
"Event-Pattern-Structure is the iceberg model in action - the practice of diving below the visible event"
"System blindness is being stuck at the event level - never reaching the pattern or structure beneath"
"Most fixes that fail address events without reaching the structure that produces them"
"System traps only become visible at the structure level - at the event level they look like isolated misfortunes"
"Feedback loops are what you find when you move from pattern to structure - the loops are what produce the recurring pattern"
"Exponential growth is a reinforcing feedback loop left unchecked"
"Exponential growth happens when a stock feeds back into its own inflow"
"Exponential growth is always the first half of an S-curve - limits always arrive"
"Exponential growth can push a system past a tipping point before anyone notices"
"Unchecked exponential growth eventually hits a limit - the archetype explains what happens when it does"
"Exponential growth and Success to the Successful share the same engine - a reinforcing loop that compounds advantage"
"Unchecked exponential growth drives overshoot - the system blows past its limits before feedback arrives"
"Exponential growth is a second-order effect in action - each cycle's output feeds the next cycle's input"
"Linear thinking is why exponential growth always surprises people - we instinctively project in straight lines"
"Network effects drive exponential growth in early stages - each new user increases value for all existing ones"
"Feedback loops work by adjusting flows, which change stocks over time"
"Oscillation is what happens when a balancing feedback loop overcorrects due to delays"
"This archetype is a reinforcing loop that compounds advantage - success generates resources that generate more success"
"Many nudges work by making feedback visible that the system was previously hiding"
"A feedback loop can only correct effectively if the system has enough variety of responses to match what it's sensing"
"Self-organisation arises through feedback loops between agents - each response shapes the next interaction"
"Mental models filter which feedback we notice and which we ignore - reinforcing what we already believe"
"Linear thinking treats feedback loops as straight lines - ignoring the circular causality that drives system behaviour"
"Resilient systems have feedback loops that detect disturbance early and trigger adaptation before damage compounds"
"Power often works through feedback loops - those with resources gain more, reinforcing their position"
"Goodhart's Law corrupts feedback loops - the signal guiding the system becomes the thing the system games"
"Leading indicators create faster feedback loops, letting the system adjust before lagging indicators confirm the damage"
"A feedback loop is only useful if it carries signal - responding to noise makes the system oscillate without improving"
"Feedback starvation is what happens when feedback loops are broken - the system loses its ability to self-correct"
"Observation creates a new feedback loop in the system - the system senses it's being watched and adjusts accordingly"
"Perverse incentives create reinforcing feedback loops that amplify the problem the incentive was supposed to solve"
"Learning organisations are built on feedback loops that translate experience into structural adaptation"
"Niche construction creates feedback between organism and environment - reshaping the world, which reshapes the organism"
"Changing a feedback loop's structure is one of the highest-leverage interventions in a system"
"Multiple balancing feedback loops pulling toward different goals is what creates policy resistance"
"Feedback starvation produces system blindness - without information, the system can't see its own dysfunction"
"Groupthink creates feedback starvation by suppressing the dissent that would provide corrective information"
"Parasitic relationships often involve feedback starvation - the host's deterioration is invisible to the parasite"
Fixes that fail often lead to shifting the burden - the quick fix becomes the default, and the real solution atrophies.
The fix creates a balancing loop that addresses the symptom, but a delayed reinforcing loop makes the root cause worse.
The delay between the fix and its side effects is what makes this pattern so dangerous - the fix looks like it works, until it doesn't.
Every fix that fails is a case study in unintended consequences.
"Fixes often fail because the boundary was drawn too tightly - the side effects sit outside the scope"
"Quick fixes often deplete buffers that the system needs for long-term stability"
"Pushing harder on growth when the constraint is the problem is a fix that fails"
"Rules designed to fix problems are beaten when people comply technically while violating the intent"
"Fixes that fail often trigger policy resistance - the system absorbs the fix and reasserts the original pattern"
"Fixes that fail is one of the most common system traps - recognisable and escapable once named"
"Most fixes that fail address the tip of the iceberg - the event - without touching the structure beneath"
"Solutionism drives fixes that fail by demanding a solution before the problem is properly understood"
"Every quick fix that avoids a structural solution adds to the stock of organisational debt"
"Goodhart's Law is what happens when a proxy measure becomes a target - people optimise the proxy, not the thing it represents"
"Goodhart's Law is the mechanism behind most perverse incentives - the measure drives behaviour away from the intended goal"
"Goodhart's Law creates a system trap - the better you get at hitting the measure, the further you drift from the goal"
"The unintended consequence of targeting a measure is that the measure stops measuring what it used to"
"The observer effect is the behavioural mechanism behind Goodhart's Law - people change behaviour when they know it's measured"
"Surrogate measures are Goodhart's Law fully realised - the measure has displaced the goal entirely"
"Groupthink is the collapse of multiple perspectives into one - the group converges prematurely on a single view"
"Groupthink creates a reinforcing loop - agreement breeds more agreement, suppressing the dissent that would break the cycle"
"Groupthink is a form of system blindness - the group can't see its own dysfunction because the dysfunction feels like unity"
"Groupthink locks in a shared mental model and prevents it from being updated, even when evidence contradicts it"
"Groupthink amplifies motivated reasoning - shared beliefs become even more resistant to disconfirming evidence"
"Growth and Underinvestment is what happens when the constraint in Limits to Growth is capacity that could have been built but wasn't"
"A vicious balancing loop: poor performance reduces demand, which seems to justify the lack of investment"
"Investment in capacity takes time to pay off - the delay makes it easy to argue the investment isn't working"
"Capacity is a stock that depletes when investment (inflow) doesn't keep pace with demand (outflow)"
"Hindsight bias feeds the narrative fallacy - once the ending is known, the story writes itself backward to make it seem inevitable"
"Hindsight bias ignores sensitivity to initial conditions - the outcome that feels inevitable was actually contingent on details nobody noticed"
"Hindsight bias converts genuine uncertainty into apparent risk - after the fact, the odds seem knowable when they weren't"
"Hindsight bias enables system blindness by making it seem like someone should have known - replacing structural analysis with blame"
"Hindsight bias reinforces mental models - it makes us think our predictions are better than they are, so we never update the model"
"Holding space creates conditions for self-organisation - order emerges when the container is right"
"Holding space is the leadership approach for complex situations - where controlling outcomes isn't possible"
"Mental models sit at the deepest level of the iceberg - the beliefs that shape the structures that produce the events"
"System blindness is being stuck at the tip of the iceberg - seeing events without seeing what's underneath"
"The iceberg shows why higher leverage points are harder to see - they're below the waterline"
"Feedback loops live in the structure layer of the iceberg - invisible from the event level but driving everything"
"Institutional inertia is path dependence at the organisational level - accumulated decisions constrain future movement"
"Lock-in is inertia's endgame - the cost of changing becomes so high that the institution is stuck even when the current path is failing"
"Organisational debt feeds inertia - the more accumulated shortcuts, the harder it is to change direction"
"Institutional inertia is a major source of policy resistance - the structure absorbs change efforts and reasserts itself"
"Feedback loops are interconnections that circle back on themselves, creating self-reinforcing or self-correcting behaviour"
"Interconnections are what make systems nonlinear - a change in one place ripples unpredictably through the web"
"Many interconnections include delays, so the effect of a change travels slowly through the system"
"Side effects travel along interconnections - the more connected the system, the wider the ripple"
"Wicked problems are wicked because they're deeply interconnected - you can't pull one thread without moving everything"
"Self-organisation requires interconnection - isolated agents can't produce collective order"
"Systems mapping is the broader practice of making interconnections visible across a whole system"
"Reductionism discards interconnections when it separates parts - and the interconnections are often where the behaviour lives"
"Modularity manages interconnections by keeping modules loosely connected - preventing cascading failure"
"Sub-optimisation ignores interconnections - improving parts without considering what flows between them"
"Silos form when interconnections between parts weaken - information and understanding stop flowing across boundaries"
"Keystone species matter because of their interconnections - they affect far more of the system than their size suggests"
"Intervention side effects are a specific case of unintended consequences - the ones caused by deliberate action"
"Side effects are often the mechanism through which policy resistance operates"
"High-leverage interventions can produce powerful side effects, good and bad - leverage amplifies everything"
"Nudges have side effects too - changing one default can shift behaviour in adjacent areas"
"Every constraint creates side effects - the behaviour it prevents has to go somewhere"
"Solutionism ignores intervention side effects because admitting them would mean the solution isn't clean"
"Safe-to-fail experiments contain intervention side effects by keeping the scale small and the commitment reversible"
"Measurement is itself an intervention - the observer effect is its unavoidable side effect"
"Systemic design anticipates intervention side effects by mapping the wider system before acting"
"Sensitivity makes systems irreducible - if tiny details cascade, you can't simplify without losing the outcome"
"Wicked problems are partly wicked because they're irreducible - you can't model your way to a solution"
"Irreducibility is a deep source of uncertainty - if you can't shortcut the calculation, you can't know the outcome in advance"
"Irreducible systems can't be fully mapped by definition - the only complete model is the system itself running in real time"
"Safe-to-fail experiments respect irreducibility - if the only way to know is to try, make the trying safe and small"
"Probing respects irreducibility - if the system can't be shortcut, interacting with it is the only way to learn"
"A system's resilience often depends on its keystone species - their removal can trigger cascading collapse"
"Keystone species occupy leverage points - their influence on the system is disproportionate to their visibility"
"Systems with keystone dependencies lack modularity - the whole depends on one critical node"
"Systems without redundancy for their keystone roles are catastrophically fragile"
"Lagging indicators often measure stocks (accumulated results). Leading indicators often measure flows (current activity)"
"Senge identified mental models as one of the five disciplines - surfacing and testing assumptions is core to organisational learning"
"Learning organisations overcome system blindness by developing the habit of looking at structures, not just events"
"Flows are low-leverage - easy to see but slow to shift. The rules governing flows are where the real leverage lives"
"Redefining what's inside the system is a high-leverage move that changes everything downstream"
"Most failed fixes target low-leverage points - adjusting numbers instead of changing structures"
"The pattern of interconnections determines where leverage exists in a system"
"Choosing the right leverage point requires second-order thinking - what happens after the first shift?"
"Nudges target a specific leverage point - the choice architecture that shapes individual decisions"
"Increasing your range of possible responses is itself a leverage point"
"Changing mental models is one of the highest leverage points in any system - near the top of Meadows' hierarchy"
"System blindness keeps people pushing on low-leverage points - blaming individuals instead of redesigning structures"
"The highest leverage points - rules, goals, paradigms - are also the points where power is most concentrated"
"A minimum viable intervention targets the leverage point - the smallest change that could shift the system"
"Limits to Growth is the structural explanation for why S-curves flatten"
"The archetype is a reinforcing loop driving growth that eventually activates a balancing loop"
"Limits often show up as buffer depletion - a resource runs low before it runs out"
"If limits aren't addressed and growth continues unchecked, Limits to Growth becomes Overshoot and Collapse"
"Limits to growth are often scale effects - the system hits constraints that didn't exist at a smaller scale"
"Linear thinking is the failure to see nonlinearity - assuming the system responds proportionally when it doesn't"
"Linear thinking stops at the first-order effect and misses everything that follows"
"Linear thinking is the most common mental model - and the most dangerous one in complex systems"
"Reductionism and linear thinking are natural partners - break it into parts, solve each one, assume they add up"
"Solutionism is linear thinking applied to problem-solving - one problem, one cause, one fix"
"Most theories of change are dangerously linear - real change doesn't follow a straight chain from activity to impact"
"The narrative fallacy imposes linear causation on nonlinear events - turning a web of causes into a chain"
"Sub-optimisation is the pathology that results when local wins take priority over global performance"
"Tragedy of the commons is local-vs-global in its purest form - rational individual choices destroying the shared resource"
"The local-global tension depends on where you draw the boundary - what counts as 'local' and what counts as 'global'"
"In nested systems, every level faces the local-vs-global tension with the levels above and below"
"Feedback loops that connect local actions to global consequences help align local and global optimisation"
"Lock-in is path dependence at its most extreme - not just constrained by history, but trapped by it"
"Reinforcing loops entrench lock-in - the more you use a system, the harder it becomes to leave"
"Lock-in accumulates through stocks of investment, learning, and integration that are lost if you switch"
"The dominant option attracts more users, more investment, more compatibility - widening the gap with alternatives"
"Lock-in during stable periods explains why change accumulates as pressure rather than gradual adjustment"
"Network effects create lock-in at the market level - switching away means losing access to the network's value"
"Every mental model is a map - a simplification that helps you navigate but never captures the full territory"
"Every systems map is a simplification - useful for seeing patterns, dangerous when mistaken for reality"
"Maps work well for complicated territory and mislead for complex territory - where the landscape shifts as you walk it"
"Reductionism creates maps by breaking things into parts - and the map always loses the interactions between parts"
"Each perspective is a map. Multiple maps together get closer to the territory than any single one"
"A proxy measure is a map of the thing you care about - useful but never the thing itself"
"System blindness is what happens when mental models can't see structure - defaulting to individual blame"
"Systems mapping surfaces mental models by forcing people to draw what they think is happening"
"Transformation requires changing mental models - the deepest, hardest, and highest-leverage kind of change"
"Each perspective carries its own mental models - its own assumptions about what's important and why"
"Sensemaking builds and updates mental models - the process by which our map of reality gets revised"
"Motivated reasoning protects mental models from being updated - we filter evidence to confirm rather than challenge"
"Narratives build and reinforce mental models - the stories we tell shape what we expect to happen next"
"Participatory mapping surfaces hidden mental models - the process of drawing together makes assumptions visible"
"MVIs are safe-to-fail by design - small enough that failure is cheap and success can be scaled"
"MVIs are the probes in probe-sense-respond - the smallest useful action that generates learning"
"Smaller interventions produce smaller side effects - the minimum viable approach limits unintended damage"
"MVIs suit complex environments where you can't predict the effect - keep the bet small until you understand the response"
"Modularity is a key design principle for resilience - failure in one module doesn't cascade to the rest"
"Modularity and redundancy work together - modular systems can have redundant components that back each other up"
"The tension: modularity protects the whole by limiting integration, but too much separation is sub-optimisation"
"Modularity helps manage complexity by containing it within units - each module can be complex internally while being simple externally"
"Silos are modularity without integration - independent parts that have lost the coordination the whole requires"
"Motivated reasoning creates a reinforcing loop - beliefs filter evidence, filtered evidence reinforces beliefs"
"Motivated reasoning sustains system blindness - if you're motivated to see individual causes, systemic ones stay invisible"
"Wicked problems look different to every stakeholder - multiple perspectives are essential, not optional"
"Systems mapping with multiple perspectives produces richer, more accurate maps than any single viewpoint"
"Sensemaking benefits from multiple perspectives because each viewpoint reveals different aspects of an ambiguous situation"
"Systemic design insists on multiple perspectives - the designer's view alone is never complete"
"Participatory mapping assembles multiple perspectives into a shared picture through the act of drawing together"
"Mutualism is the positive form of symbiosis - both parties are better off because of the relationship"
"Mutualistic partners co-evolve toward deeper interdependence - each becoming more valuable to the other"
"Mutualism is sustained by reinforcing feedback loops - each party's benefit reinforces the relationship"
"Network effects can create mutualism at scale - platforms and users benefiting from each other's participation"
"True mutualism aligns local and global optimisation - what's good for each party is good for the whole"
"The narrative fallacy ignores sensitivity to initial conditions - the story makes the outcome seem inevitable when it was contingent"
"The narrative fallacy reduces complex events to complicated ones - a neat story with clear causes and effects"
"Panarchy is nested systems in motion - each level going through its own adaptive cycle, affecting the others"
"What works at one level of a nested system often doesn't work at another - scale changes the rules"
"Sub-optimisation in nested systems means one level optimising at the expense of the levels above or below"
"Network effects are a reinforcing feedback loop - more users make the system more valuable, which attracts more users"
"Network effects are success-to-the-successful at the platform level - the biggest network gets more users, which makes it bigger"
"Network effects have a tipping point - once the network is large enough, growth becomes self-sustaining"
"In complex adaptive systems, agents don't just respond to the environment - they construct it"
"Niche construction creates path dependence - the environment you've built constrains the options you have"
"Reinforcing feedback loops are the engine of nonlinearity, turning small changes into large ones"
"Exponential growth is one of the most dramatic forms of nonlinear behaviour"
"Tipping points are where nonlinearity is most dramatic - a tiny push triggers a massive shift"
"S-curves show nonlinearity in action - slow start, explosive middle, plateau at the end"
"Second-order effects can be wildly disproportionate to the first-order cause - nonlinearity across time"
"Sensitivity to initial conditions is nonlinearity in the time dimension - small early differences amplified into large later ones"
"Scale effects are a form of nonlinearity - doubling the size doesn't produce double the outcome, it changes the system's nature"
"Nudges can produce second-order effects - changing one default can shift behaviour patterns well beyond the original choice"
"Nudges often meet less resistance than mandates because they don't restrict freedom - they redirect attention"
"The observer effect means that measuring a proxy doesn't just simplify reality - it changes reality"
"Organisational debt is a stock that accumulates when the inflow of shortcuts exceeds the outflow of structural fixes"
"Buffers dampen oscillation by absorbing the overshoot before it triggers a panicked correction"
"Oscillation is visible in stocks - the level swings above and below where it needs to be"
"Outcome mapping operationalises the theory of change by focusing on what changes in the people the programme influences"
"Outcome mapping builds feedback into change work - tracking behavioural shifts gives you earlier signals than waiting for final outcomes"
"Outcome mapping tracks the first-order effects (behaviour change) that produce second-order effects (system change)"
"Outcome mapping pairs well with safe-to-fail experiments - track what changes, amplify what works, dampen what doesn't"
"Delays are the reason overshoot happens - the signal to stop arrives after the damage is done"
"Overshoot can push a system past a tipping point, making the collapse irreversible"
"Overshoot drains a stock faster than it can replenish, until the stock collapses"
"Panarchy explains how resilience operates across scales - a resilient higher level can absorb disruption at lower levels"
"Panarchy explains how small-scale tipping points can cascade upward and trigger system-wide transitions"
"Regime shifts in panarchy happen when a release phase at one scale triggers collapse at a higher scale"
"Parasitism is the extractive form of symbiosis - one party benefits, the other is weakened"
"Parasitism is the opposite of mutualism - one party's gain comes at the other's direct expense"
"Parasitic relationships often reinforce themselves through success-to-the-successful dynamics - the extracting party grows stronger"
"Parasitic relationships are sustained by power imbalances - the exploited party can't exit or renegotiate"
"Participatory mapping is systems mapping done with the people inside the system - producing richer, more accurate maps"
"Who participates in the mapping determines what the map shows - stakeholder inclusion is a design choice"
"Participatory mapping is collective sensemaking - the group constructs shared understanding through the process of drawing together"
"Reinforcing feedback loops create path dependence by amplifying early choices until they become entrenched"
"Early decisions can push a system past tipping points that lock it onto one path"
"Past boundary choices constrain where future boundaries can be drawn"
"Every past decision leaves deposits in stocks - skills, infrastructure, culture - that shape what's possible next"
"Early advantages become path-dependent as the reinforcing loop entrenches the winner's position"
"Sensitivity to initial conditions is why paths diverge so dramatically from nearly identical starting points"
"Perverse incentives are a specific type of unintended consequence - the incentive drives behaviour opposite to the intent"
"Perverse incentives are a system trap - the structure reliably produces the wrong behaviour regardless of who's in the system"
"Policy resistance is the sign you've hit a low-leverage point - the structure is stronger than your push"
"The system's resistance often manifests as unintended consequences that cancel out the intended ones"
"When resistance makes a real solution too hard, the temptation is to shift the burden to something easier"
"Policy resistance often means the intervention lacks enough variety to handle the system's complexity"
"A portfolio of experiments is a collection of safe-to-fail probes - each one small, together they cover more ground"
"The portfolio approach runs multiple probes simultaneously rather than sequentially - faster learning"
"A portfolio of experiments increases requisite variety - more approaches mean more chances of matching what the system needs"
"Portfolios suit complex situations where you can't predict which intervention will work - diversify the bets"
"Stakeholder mapping reveals power dynamics - who has influence, who has interest, and who's invisible"
"Success to the successful is power made structural - winners get more resources, which makes them win more"
"Safe-to-fail experiments are how probes work in practice - small, bounded, designed for learning"
"Probe-sense-respond suits complex situations where analysis can't predict the outcome - the system must be tested"
"Probe-sense-respond is for complex domains. Sense-analyse-respond is for complicated ones. Using the wrong one is expensive"
"Proxy measures can serve as leading indicators - early, measurable signals of something harder to track"
"Proxy measures are reductionism applied to measurement - simplifying a complex reality into a single number"
"The choice of proxy reveals a mental model - what you measure is a statement about what you believe matters"
"Surrogate measures are proxies that have replaced the original goal - the stand-in became the purpose"
"Regime shifts are the punctuation in punctuated equilibrium - the sudden breaks between long stable periods"
"Tipping points are the trigger mechanism for the punctuation - the moment when gradual pressure produces sudden change"
"Sub-optimisation is reductionism in action - optimising each part separately and assuming the whole will improve"
"Sense-analyse-respond relies on reductionism - breaking the problem into parts that experts can diagnose"
"Redundancy is one of the primary building blocks of resilience - the spare capacity that absorbs shocks"
"Redundancy serves both strategies - robust systems use it to prevent failure, resilient systems use it to recover from it"
"Tipping points are the trigger for regime shifts - the moment when gradual change becomes a sudden flip"
"Regime shifts happen when resilience is exhausted - the system can no longer absorb disturbance and flips"
"Regime shifts are preceded by weak signals - declining resilience, slower recovery, and increasing variability"
"Transition management aims to guide regime shifts intentionally rather than leaving them to crisis"
"Constraints reduce the variety a system needs to manage - they simplify the problem space"
"Robustness resists change; resilience absorbs it. Different strategies for different situations"
"Sometimes resilience means maintaining the current system. Transformability is knowing when to let it go and build something new"
"Safe-to-fail experiments build resilience by helping systems learn without being damaged by the learning process"
"Buffers serve both strategies - absorbing known shocks (robustness) and buying time to adapt to unknown ones (resilience)"
"Rules create feedback loops that shape behaviour - but the behaviour shaped isn't always the behaviour intended"
"Rule beating is the unintended consequence of well-intentioned rules that target the wrong thing"
"Rules draw boundaries around what's measured and enforced - rule beating exploits what's outside those boundaries"
"S-curves are shaped by reinforcing loops that drive growth and balancing loops that slow it"
"The curve traces a stock that grows quickly when inflows dominate, then plateaus as outflows catch up"
"In complex systems, satisficing is often the only viable strategy - the optimal choice can't be identified in advance"
"In irreducible systems, the optimal choice can't be calculated - satisficing is the honest response"
"Constraints that force satisficing can improve decisions by eliminating the paralysis of seeking the optimal"
"Satisficing is the antidote to solutionism - accepting that good enough IS good enough in complex situations"
"Scaling often pushes systems from complicated into complex territory - more agents, more interactions, more emergence"
"Scale changes the local-vs-global calculus - what counts as 'local' and 'global' shifts as the system grows"
"Side effects are first-order ripples; second-order effects are what those ripples cause in turn"
"Most unintended consequences are second- or third-order effects that weren't traced far enough forward"
"Second-order effects are a major source of genuine uncertainty - the consequences of consequences resist prediction"
"Succession is self-organisation after disruption - the system rebuilds itself through predictable stages"
"This approach works for complicated problems where experts can find the answer through analysis"
"This approach works when the system is sufficiently linear - when expert analysis can trace cause to effect"
"Sensemaking under pressure is the compressed, high-stakes version - pattern recognition replaces deliberate analysis"
"Pressure tightens the bounds of rationality further - less time, less information, more stress"
"Pressure increases cognitive load, forcing reliance on pattern recognition and trained responses"
"Pressure amplifies action bias - the urge to do something immediately, even when waiting would be smarter"
"Wicked problems demand sensemaking because they resist the standard problem-solving sequence of define, analyse, solve"
"Sensitive dependence is a major source of genuine uncertainty - if tiny unknowns matter, prediction becomes impossible"
"The reinforcing loop between quick-fix reliance and real-solution atrophy drives the pattern"
"The real solution usually has a longer delay, which makes the quick fix more attractive"
"The dependency emerges gradually from repeated small choices, not from a single decision"
"The capacity for the real solution is a stock that atrophies when the burden keeps getting shifted"
"The symptomatic fix often drains buffers that the fundamental solution needs"
"Both archetypes involve structural incentives that make the individually rational choice collectively harmful"
"Shifting the burden is a system trap - the reliance on the easy fix is structural, not a character flaw"
"Leading indicators are noisier than lagging ones - the trade-off between timeliness and signal clarity"
"Weak signals are real signal buried in noise - the challenge is detecting them before they become obvious"
"In nonlinear systems, distinguishing signal from noise is harder because small causes can produce large effects"
"Silos are the organisational structure that produces sub-optimisation - each silo optimises locally at the expense of the whole"
"Silos break feedback loops between parts of the system - each part stops sensing what's happening in the others"
"Solutionism is the refusal to accept that wicked problems exist - treating every messy situation as a puzzle to be solved"
"Stakeholder mapping is boundary critique in practice - deciding who's inside the system and whose interests count"
"Each stakeholder brings a different perspective - the map shows whose views you need to include"
"Stakeholder mapping is one form of systems mapping - focused on the human actors and their relationships to the system"
"Unintended consequences often land on stakeholders who weren't mapped - the people nobody thought to include"
"Stock and flow diagrams are a key systems mapping technique - tracking what accumulates and what moves through the system"
"A small initial advantage can produce a massively disproportionate outcome over time"
"Succession shows that a system doesn't return to its previous state immediately - it passes through distinct phases"
"Succession is what happens during the stability phase after a punctuation - the system rebuilds through stages"
"A system's capacity for succession IS its resilience - the ability to regenerate after disturbance"
"Surrogate measures reshape mental models - people start believing the surrogate IS the goal, not a stand-in for it"
"Symbiosis is interconnection at its deepest - two systems whose functioning depends on each other"
"Network effects can create symbiotic relationships between platforms and users - each needs the other"
"System traps only become visible when you overcome system blindness - the pattern looks like bad luck until you see the structure"
"Linear thinking feeds system blindness - if cause and effect are simple and direct, the cause must be a person"
"The trap where rational individual behaviour destroys the shared resource everyone depends on"
"Understanding system traps helps explain why policy resistance occurs - you're fighting a structural pattern"
"Adding an enabling constraint is often the escape route from a system trap"
"Systems mapping is a core tool of systemic design - making the system visible before designing the intervention"
"Systemic design was developed for wicked problems - situations where traditional design can't grasp the full picture"
"A good theory of change is a systems map - showing feedback, delays, and multiple pathways, not just a logic chain"
"A theory of change makes your mental model of how change works explicit and testable"
"Theories of change for wicked problems need to be adaptive, not fixed - the pathway changes as the context does"
"A good theory of change traces second-order effects - what happens after the first outcome, not just the outcome itself"
"Tipping points often trigger reinforcing feedback loops that accelerate the change and make it irreversible"
"The steep middle of an S-curve is what it looks like after a tipping point has been crossed"
"Delays can hide how close a system is to its tipping point until it's too late to pull back"
"Weak signals often precede tipping points - early tremors that announce a shift before it becomes irreversible"
"Each user's reinforcing loop (more use = more benefit) overwhelms the balancing loop of the shared resource"
"The commons is a stock being drained by multiple outflows faster than it can replenish"
"The tragedy happens because individual boundaries don't include the shared resource - each person sees only their own costs and benefits"
"The commons can become an escalation where each party's use of the resource provokes others to use more before it's gone"
"The commons has a carrying capacity - growth in use eventually hits the limit of what the resource can sustain"
"Transformability is the deliberate version of a regime shift - choosing to build a new system rather than being forced into one"
"Transition management is the governance framework for exercising transformability at societal scale"
"Transition management was designed for wicked problems that require multi-decade, multi-stakeholder approaches"
"Transition management uses experiments at the 'niche' level to test innovations before scaling them into the regime"
"Nonlinearity generates uncertainty because disproportionate effects can't be predicted from proportional models"
"Many unintended consequences are delayed - they arrive long after the action that caused them"
"Unintended consequences often travel through feedback loops you didn't know existed"
"Interconnections carry effects through the system in ways that surprise us"
"Nonlinearity means a small intervention can produce outsized, unexpected side effects"
"Every attempt to address a wicked problem produces unintended consequences that reshape the problem itself"
"Detecting weak signals is a sensemaking practice - it requires attention to ambiguity rather than certainty"
"Wicked problems arise from complex adaptive systems - the agents' responses to your solution become part of the problem"
